meta.title: CCA-F-4: Agent Reliability, MCP, and Claude Code Operations

questions.stem: A team is deciding whether every request must go through a planner, tool runner, and reviewer chain. When is the simpler design usually the better choice?

options.text: When the task is straightforward and can be answered directly without tools
When the team wants to maximize orchestration overhead
When every request must be decomposed even if it is trivial
When latency and cost are irrelevant

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Anthropic guidance recommends starting with the simplest pattern that meets the task and only adding agent loops when they materially help.

**B** is incorrect because Extra orchestration adds latency, cost, and failure points rather than value by itself.

**C** is incorrect because Forcing decomposition on trivial work creates unnecessary complexity.

**D** is incorrect because Latency and cost still matter in production design decisions.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem: A support bot must summarize a user message and answer from static policy text that is already in the prompt. Which architecture is the best fit?

options.text: A direct prompt-response workflow with no tool call
A manager-worker multi-agent hierarchy
A tool loop that always calls three external systems
A peer-agent swarm that debates every answer

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because A direct workflow is sufficient because the task does not require tool use or autonomous multi-step planning.

**B** is incorrect because A hierarchy is heavier than the use case requires.

**C** is incorrect because Mandatory external calls add avoidable latency and operational risk.

**D** is incorrect because A swarm does not improve a simple deterministic lookup-and-answer task.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem: A team breaks a long task into retrieval, planning, and execution subtasks that can run independently before a final synthesis step. What is the main benefit of that design?

options.text: It can reduce context overload and let the system parallelize parts of the work
It guarantees zero hallucinations
It removes the need for validation or monitoring
It prevents the model from ever needing tools

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Task decomposition can isolate context, improve controllability, and allow parallelism when subtasks are independent.

**B** is incorrect because No orchestration pattern can guarantee zero hallucinations.

**C** is incorrect because Validation and monitoring remain necessary for reliability.

**D** is incorrect because Some decomposed tasks still need tools or retrieval.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem: An orchestrator agent sends specialized subtasks to separate research, policy, and formatting agents, then merges their outputs. Which pattern is this?

options.text: Hub-and-spoke orchestration with specialist subagents
Pure single-turn prompting
A cache-control optimization
A tool schema collision

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because This is the classic orchestrator pattern in which one controller delegates work to specialists and combines results.

**B** is incorrect because Single-turn prompting does not involve separate specialist agents.

**C** is incorrect because Caching is unrelated to task delegation.

**D** is incorrect because Tool name collisions are a separate MCP design concern.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem: Claude chooses whether to call a tool partly from the tool description. What makes a tool description most useful?

options.text: Clear purpose, input expectations, and when the tool should be used
A very short name with no description
Ambiguous wording so the model can improvise
Internal implementation details that never affect invocation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Good descriptions help Claude match user intent to the right tool and populate inputs correctly.

**B** is incorrect because A name alone is usually insufficient for reliable selection.

**C** is incorrect because Ambiguity harms invocation accuracy.

**D** is incorrect because Implementation detail is less important than intent, inputs, and side effects.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem: Which statement about tool execution in Anthropic tool use is correct?

options.text: Claude returns a structured tool call and the application or server executes the tool
Claude directly logs into external systems without any tool definition
Tool use only works when every tool is hosted by Anthropic
A tool call means the final answer is already guaranteed to be correct

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Anthropic documents that Claude emits structured tool calls while execution happens in your application for client tools or on Anthropic-managed server tools.

**B** is incorrect because Claude needs explicit tool definitions and does not autonomously bypass them.

**C** is incorrect because Client tools are supported; Anthropic hosting is not required for all tools.

**D** is incorrect because A tool call can still fail or return bad inputs, so validation is still required.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem: An MCP server needs to give Claude read-only access to a library of product documentation articles. Claude should be able to browse and read the articles but cannot trigger any function calls. Which MCP primitive is designed for this use case?

options.text: Tools — because tools are used whenever Claude needs to retrieve external content
Resources — because resources expose structured, addressable content for Claude to read without invoking a function
Prompts — because prompts inject contextual instructions alongside the content
Streams — because real-time content delivery requires a streaming primitive

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because tools are designed for functions Claude can invoke to perform actions or retrieve dynamic data. Read-only structured content that does not require a function call is better exposed as a resource.

**B** is correct because MCP resources represent structured, addressable data — such as files, database records, or documentation articles — that Claude can read without triggering a function invocation. Resources are the appropriate primitive for browsable, static or semi-static content.

**C** is incorrect because prompts are reusable instruction templates that operators attach to conversations to configure Claude's behavior. They are not designed for exposing document content.

**D** is incorrect because MCP does not define a 'Streams' primitive. The three MCP primitives are tools, resources, and prompts.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem: Two MCP servers each expose a tool called `search`. What is the best design response?

options.text: Namespace or otherwise disambiguate the tools so invocation stays predictable
Rely on random selection between the two tools
Remove every description field from both tools
Force every request through both tools whether needed or not

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Disambiguation avoids collisions and makes selection reliable when multiple servers expose similar capabilities.

**B** is incorrect because Random selection is unreliable and hard to debug.

**C** is incorrect because Descriptions improve selection rather than harming it.

**D** is incorrect because Mandatory fan-out increases cost and can confuse downstream reasoning.

[MCP: Getting started](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro)

questions.stem: Which prompt element belongs in the system prompt rather than being repeated in every user message?

options.text: Global role, safety constraints, and output rules that should persist across turns
A one-off follow-up question from the user
The temporary contents of a single file upload
A random seed value for retries

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because The system prompt is the right place for persistent role and policy guidance.

**B** is incorrect because A one-off user request belongs in the user turn.

**C** is incorrect because Ephemeral task data belongs in context, not durable policy instructions.

**D** is incorrect because Retry randomness is not a system-prompt concept.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem: A team wants more consistent structured JSON output from Claude. Which technique helps most directly?

options.text: Provide an explicit schema or format example and validate-and-retry if needed
Increase ambiguity so the model has freedom
Hide the expected keys until after generation
Replace all examples with unrelated prose

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Anthropic recommends making the desired format explicit and using validation loops for consistency-sensitive outputs.

**B** is incorrect because Ambiguity reduces consistency.

**C** is incorrect because Delayed format disclosure works against schema adherence.

**D** is incorrect because Unrelated prose examples do not anchor the required structure.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem: A prompt already contains detailed instructions. Why would a few-shot example still help?

options.text: It demonstrates the target pattern or tone in a concrete form the model can imitate
It disables the need for a system prompt
It increases the context window size
It guarantees that unsafe content will be accepted

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Few-shot examples provide an in-context pattern for formatting and reasoning style.

**B** is incorrect because Examples complement rather than replace a good system prompt.

**C** is incorrect because Examples consume context; they do not increase the window.

**D** is incorrect because Safety policies are not bypassed by examples.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem: A workflow must be as deterministic as practical for a grading rubric. Which parameter choice is best aligned with that goal?

options.text: Randomly changing the schema each run
Lower temperature and stable formatting instructions
Higher temperature and minimal instructions
Higher temperature with extra stylistic variation

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Lower temperature plus explicit formatting usually improves repeatability.

**C** is incorrect because Higher temperature generally increases variation.

**D** is incorrect because Extra variation works against deterministic grading.

**A** is incorrect because Changing schemas destroys consistency.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem: A conversation is becoming very long and expensive. What is the best first reliability move before the context window is exhausted?

options.text: Disable retrieval and grounding
Summarize or compact stale context while keeping the facts needed for future turns
Duplicate the entire conversation in every tool call
Remove the latest user message

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Compaction preserves important state while reducing token load.

**C** is incorrect because Duplication increases cost and token pressure.

**D** is incorrect because Removing the latest request loses the current task.

**A** is incorrect because Disabling grounding often worsens answer quality.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem: A team stores a compact handoff object between sessions. What problem does that solve best?

options.text: Eliminating the need to monitor tool failures
Restoring relevant state without replaying the entire conversation
Increasing model parameter count
Avoiding every need for user authentication

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because A handoff summary or state object lets a later session continue work without replaying every prior token.

**C** is incorrect because State transfer does not affect model size.

**D** is incorrect because Authentication is a separate system concern.

**A** is incorrect because Tool failures still need operational handling.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem: Prompt caching is most valuable when which condition is true?

options.text: Only tool outputs are cached by the external database
A large repeated prompt prefix is reused across many requests
Every request uses completely different context
The application never sends long prompts

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Prompt caching reduces cost and latency when large prompt segments repeat across calls.

**C** is incorrect because No reuse means little cache benefit.

**D** is incorrect because Short prompts create less opportunity for cache savings.

**A** is incorrect because Prompt caching is about repeated prompt segments, not only external DB tool outputs.

[Prompt caching - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching)

questions.stem: An agent is unsure whether a fact is grounded in retrieved context or inferred. What is the safest response pattern?

options.text: Suppress every answer that includes uncertainty
Calibrate confidence, cite the retrieval, and ask to verify when support is weak
State the guess confidently to keep the conversation moving
Invent a source that looks authoritative

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Confidence calibration and explicit verification requests reduce hallucination risk when evidence is weak.

**C** is incorrect because Overconfident guessing is unsafe.

**D** is incorrect because Fabricated sources are unacceptable.

**A** is incorrect because Blank refusal is not always necessary when the system can state limits and verify.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem: A repository contains project-specific Claude Code instructions that should apply to all contributors in that repo. Which file is the most appropriate place?

options.text: A tool result artifact
A repo-level `CLAUDE.md` file
A one-off slash command response
A random shell history file

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Claude Code uses `CLAUDE.md` files to provide persistent scoped instructions, including repo-level guidance.

**C** is incorrect because A slash command response is ephemeral.

**D** is incorrect because Shell history is not a supported configuration mechanism.

**A** is incorrect because Tool results are not durable instruction files.

[Claude Code settings](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/settings)

questions.stem: What is a practical use of Claude Code slash commands?

options.text: Replacing Git entirely
Packaging a repeatable prompt or workflow behind a short command
Changing the model weights in place
Bypassing permission controls permanently

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Slash commands are designed to encapsulate reusable prompts or workflows.

**C** is incorrect because Slash commands do not alter model weights.

**D** is incorrect because They do not remove permission systems.

**A** is incorrect because Git remains the version control system.

[Claude Code slash commands](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/slash-commands)

questions.stem: A team wants to run a shell script automatically before or after certain Claude Code tool events. Which feature is designed for that?

options.text: Prompt caching
Hooks
Few-shot prompting
Temperature tuning

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Hooks exist for pre-tool and post-tool automation around Claude Code workflows.

**C** is incorrect because Few-shot prompting changes model behavior, not tool event automation.

**D** is incorrect because Temperature tuning changes randomness.

**A** is incorrect because Prompt caching does not trigger shell scripts.

[Claude Code hooks](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks)

questions.stem: Why would a team use isolated worktrees when running parallel Claude Code tasks?

options.text: To guarantee that tool permissions are never needed
To reduce branch conflicts and keep separate task contexts isolated
To make every task share the same uncommitted files
To disable version control history

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Separate worktrees help parallel tasks avoid stomping on each other while preserving independent task context.

**C** is incorrect because Sharing a single mutable working tree increases conflicts.

**D** is incorrect because Worktrees are still part of Git history.

**A** is incorrect because Permissions remain a separate control.

[Claude Code GitHub Actions](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/github-actions)

questions.stem:
A multi-agent system has six agents that all need access to a shared inventory counter. Agent A decrements the counter when it reserves stock; Agent B increments it when stock is released. They run concurrently.

Which coordination mechanism prevents race conditions?

options.text: Run agents sequentially so only one modifies the counter at a time
Give each agent its own copy of the counter and merge at the end
Use a shared external state store (e.g., a database with atomic operations) that serialises reads and writes
Have all agents send messages to each other when they modify the counter

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because separate counter copies will diverge — each agent's view of available stock becomes stale the moment another agent modifies the shared counter.

**C** is correct because a shared external state store with atomic operations (compare-and-swap, transactions) is the standard mechanism for safe concurrent state modification. Only one operation executes at a time at the database level, eliminating race conditions.

**D** is incorrect because peer messaging creates a complex coordination problem that is prone to deadlock and message ordering issues.

**A** is incorrect because sequential execution eliminates concurrency benefits. The correct fix is safe concurrent access, not serialised execution.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A pipeline calls a `lookup_inventory` tool. The tool is unavailable and returns an error. Claude's API response includes a tool result block with the error message but without a special flag. Claude ignores the error and attempts to proceed as if the lookup succeeded.

What should the tool result include to signal to Claude that an error occurred?

options.text: Set `type: "error"` in the tool result block alongside the error message
Return an empty string — Claude infers an error from missing content
Set `is_error: true` in the tool result block so Claude knows to handle it as an error rather than a successful result
Include the word 'ERROR' at the start of the content string — Claude detects this keyword

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `type: "error"` is not the tool result field used to signal errors. Tool results use `is_error` for this purpose.

**B** is incorrect because an empty string does not signal an error condition — Claude cannot distinguish an empty success from a silent failure.

**C** is correct because the `is_error: true` field in a tool result block explicitly signals to Claude that the tool call failed. Claude then knows to handle the result as an error — it can retry, use a fallback, or report the failure to the user — rather than treating the error message as a successful result.

**D** is incorrect because Claude does not detect errors by scanning the content string for keywords like 'ERROR'. The `is_error` field is the designated mechanism.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A pipeline generates a purchase order and then sends it to a payment processor. Before Claude calls the `submit_payment` tool, the orchestrator should perform a final check.

Which check is the most important safety gate?

options.text: Verify that the purchase order JSON is valid (parses without errors)
Check that Claude's confidence score for the payment is above 0.9
Confirm with a human approver that the amount, recipient, and payment reference are correct before submitting
Run the payment through a staging environment to test the tool call

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because valid JSON syntax does not mean the payment details (amount, recipient) are correct.

**B** is incorrect because Claude does not produce a 'confidence score' for tool calls in the Anthropic API.

**C** is correct because submitting a payment is an irreversible action with financial consequences. Before executing, the human-in-the-loop principle requires a human approver to verify the critical fields — amount, recipient, and reference — are correct. This is the highest-value safety check.

**D** is incorrect because a staging environment test may process a real payment if the tool is not properly isolated, and does not confirm the details are correct for the actual intended payment.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A team runs 10,000 independent document classification tasks. Processing time is not critical — results are needed by the next morning. Using the synchronous Messages API takes 8 hours and costs $200.

Which alternative reduces both cost and allows the team to submit all 10,000 tasks at once?

options.text: Use streaming to receive classifications as they are generated, reducing wait time
Chain all 10,000 documents into a single Messages API request to reduce overhead
Submit all 10,000 requests via the Message Batches API, which offers 50% off input and output token pricing for asynchronous batch processing
Use a smaller model for classification to reduce cost and processing time

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because streaming delivers tokens faster within a single request but does not batch multiple requests or reduce per-token cost.

**B** is incorrect because concatenating 10,000 documents would exceed context window limits and is not how the Messages API works.

**C** is correct because the Message Batches API is designed for large volumes of independent, asynchronous tasks. The 50% token pricing discount directly reduces cost (from $200 to ~$100), and all 10,000 tasks can be submitted in one batch call.

**D** is incorrect because a smaller model changes output quality, not just cost. The Batches API provides cost reduction while keeping the same model.

[Message Batches API - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/message-batches)

questions.stem:
A pipeline uses Claude to generate structured output in a custom XML format. The output must end with `</report>`. Claude sometimes adds text after the closing tag.

Which API parameter ensures Claude stops generating immediately after `</report>`?

options.text: Use a PostToolUse hook to trim any text after the closing tag
Add 'Do not write anything after </report>' to the system prompt
Set `stop_sequences: ["</report>"]` in the API request so Claude halts when it produces that string
Set temperature to 0 so Claude generates shorter, more predictable output

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because text instructions reduce but do not eliminate post-tag content — Claude may still occasionally add a summary after the tag.

**C** is correct because `stop_sequences` is the API parameter designed for this purpose. When Claude produces the specified string, generation halts immediately. This is a hard boundary enforced at the API level, not by Claude's instruction-following.

**D** is incorrect because temperature 0 makes output deterministic but does not stop generation at a specific string.

**A** is incorrect because post-processing in a hook works but is more fragile than the built-in stop_sequences mechanism.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
A data extraction system prompt describes what the model SHOULD do but includes no examples of common extraction errors to avoid. Extraction quality is 82%.

Which prompt addition is most likely to improve accuracy on the most common error types?

options.text: Remove the 'should do' instructions and rely only on examples
Ask Claude to 'double-check each field before returning' at the end of the prompt
Add more detail to the 'should do' description so the instructions are longer
Add negative examples (showing wrong extraction with an annotation explaining why it is wrong) for each of the top error types

questions.explanation:
**C** is incorrect because longer 'should do' instructions may not address the specific failure modes — Claude already follows the positive instructions 82% of the time.

**D** is correct because negative examples directly target the known failure modes. Showing a wrong extraction alongside an explanation of why it is wrong provides Claude with a reference for the exact patterns to avoid, calibrating it on the specific error types the team has identified.

**A** is incorrect because removing positive instructions leaves Claude without the full specification of what good extraction looks like.

**B** is incorrect because 'double-check' is a meta-instruction that does not give Claude additional information about what to check for.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A user asks a financial assistant: 'Should I invest in the tech sector?' The system prompt instructs Claude to provide financial information but not personalized investment advice.

What is the most appropriate response structure?

options.text: Provide a general overview of the tech sector and explain that personalized investment advice requires a licensed financial advisor
Refuse to answer and redirect the user to a different platform
Provide a personalized investment recommendation since the user's intent is clear
Ask 10 clarifying questions before any response to ensure the advice is personalized

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the response stays within the system prompt's scope (providing general financial information) while acknowledging the limitation (personalized advice requires a licensed professional). This is helpful, honest, and compliant with the system prompt constraints.

**B** is incorrect because refusing entirely and redirecting is unnecessarily restrictive — the system prompt allows general information, which is valuable to the user.

**C** is incorrect because providing personalized investment advice violates the explicit system prompt constraint. The system prompt constraint must be honoured.

**D** is incorrect because 10 clarifying questions without any initial response is poor user experience and does not address the user's immediate question.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A math tutoring assistant needs to solve problems that require multiple sequential reasoning steps. Users report that Claude sometimes skips steps and arrives at wrong answers.

Which prompting technique most directly improves step-by-step accuracy?

options.text: Use a higher temperature for more creative problem-solving approaches
Show the final answer in the system prompt and ask Claude to verify it
Increase max_tokens to give Claude more space for each answer
Add chain-of-thought guidance: 'Work through the problem step by step, showing each calculation before stating the final answer'

questions.explanation:
**C** is incorrect because max_tokens grants output space but does not instruct Claude to use that space for explicit step-by-step reasoning.

**D** is correct because chain-of-thought prompting explicitly instructs Claude to externalise its reasoning process. Showing each calculation step before stating the final answer forces Claude to work through the problem in a verifiable way, reducing errors caused by implicit leaps.

**A** is incorrect because higher temperature increases randomness — the opposite of what is needed for accurate multi-step arithmetic.

**B** is incorrect because showing the final answer and asking for verification does not produce correct step-by-step working.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
A developer wants to view the transcript of their current Claude Code session — all the messages, tool calls, and responses — to debug an unexpected behaviour.

Where does Claude Code store the session transcript by default?

options.text: In the terminal's scroll-back buffer — there is no persistent transcript file
In `~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/<session-id>.jsonl` — each session is logged as a JSON lines file
In the project root as `claude-session.log`
Claude Code does not store transcripts — sessions are ephemeral

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Claude Code does persist session transcripts beyond the terminal buffer.

**B** is correct because Claude Code stores session transcripts in `~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/` as JSONL files. Each session has its own file containing all messages, tool calls, and results — useful for debugging, auditing, or resuming sessions.

**C** is incorrect because Claude Code does not create a `claude-session.log` file in the project root.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code does store transcripts persistently in the user's home directory.

[Overview - Claude Code Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)

questions.stem:
A team wants to enforce that every Python file Claude Code creates in their project must pass a Black formatter check before being committed. Currently, Claude Code sometimes writes Python that fails Black.

Which mechanism enforces this automatically without requiring Claude Code to check manually?

options.text: Configure Black as a pre-commit hook in the git configuration — Claude Code will respect git hooks
Ask Claude Code to run `black .` at the end of every session
Add 'Always format Python files with Black before finishing' to CLAUDE.md
Create a PostToolUse hook that runs `black --check` on every file written by Claude Code's Write or Edit tool, returning non-zero exit code if formatting fails

questions.explanation:
**C** is incorrect because CLAUDE.md guidance instructs Claude but does not deterministically enforce the formatting check — Claude may forget or omit it.

**D** is correct because a PostToolUse hook runs automatically after every Write or Edit tool call. When `black --check` exits non-zero (formatting fails), Claude Code receives the error and can re-format the file before continuing. This is deterministic and automatic.

**A** is incorrect because git pre-commit hooks run when the developer commits, not immediately when Claude Code writes a file. They would catch the error too late in the workflow.

**B** is incorrect because asking Claude Code to run Black at session end requires remembering to do so and does not catch individual file problems as they are created.

[Claude Code Hooks](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks)

questions.stem:
A company has three internal teams using Claude Code: frontend (React), backend (Go), and data science (Python). Each team has different code style conventions, linting tools, and preferred test commands.

What is the most maintainable way to configure team-specific conventions for each project?

options.text: One global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` listing all three teams' conventions
Each project has its own CLAUDE.md at the project root with that team's conventions
Each developer maintains personal CLAUDE.md files with their individual preferences
All conventions are communicated to Claude Code through user messages at the start of each session

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because combining all three teams' conventions in one global file creates a long, potentially conflicting instruction set that applies everywhere, even when irrelevant.

**B** is correct because project-level CLAUDE.md files scope instructions to the relevant project. The React project's CLAUDE.md lists ESLint and npm test; the Go project's lists golangci-lint and go test; the Python project's lists Black and pytest. Developers work in the right context automatically.

**C** is incorrect because personal files apply the same individual preferences to all projects regardless of team context.

**D** is incorrect because repeating conventions in user messages every session is error-prone and not scalable.

[Claude Code: Memory](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory)

questions.stem:
A developer wants Claude Code to output its response as structured JSON instead of conversational text, for use in a bash script that processes the output programmatically.

Which flag achieves this?

options.text: `--format json` — outputs the response as a JSON object
`-j` — JSON shorthand flag for Claude Code
`--output-format json` — changes Claude Code's output format to JSON for programmatic consumption
Claude Code always outputs plain text; JSON formatting must be done by the calling script

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `--format` is not the correct flag name in Claude Code.

**B** is incorrect because `-j` is not a recognised Claude Code flag.

**C** is correct because `--output-format json` is the Claude Code flag that switches the output format to structured JSON, suitable for programmatic consumption in scripts and pipelines.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code does provide a JSON output mode — it does not always output plain text only.

[Overview - Claude Code Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)

questions.stem:
An MCP server for an e-commerce platform needs to give Claude access to product pricing. The pricing data changes every 10 minutes and Claude should always see the current price. Claude needs to read prices but must never modify them.

Which MCP primitive is most appropriate?

options.text: System prompt — include the full price list in the system prompt and update it every 10 minutes
MCP Tool — define a `get_price` function that returns current pricing data when called
MCP Resource with subscription support — expose each product's price as a subscribable resource that pushes updates when prices change
MCP Prompt — embed current pricing in a prompt template that is re-generated every 10 minutes

questions.explanation:
**B** is incorrect because a Tool invocation has no side effects here but works — however, it requires Claude to actively poll for price updates, which is less efficient than server-pushed updates for frequently changing data.

**C** is correct because MCP Resources with subscription support are designed for read-only, addressable data that changes over time. The server pushes notifications when prices change, allowing Claude to see current pricing without polling — and the read-only nature matches the requirement that Claude must never modify prices.

**D** is incorrect because prompt templates are injected once at conversation start and do not update dynamically during a session.

**A** is incorrect because updating the system prompt every 10 minutes requires application-level coordination outside the MCP protocol and is not scalable.

[MCP: Resources](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/resources)

questions.stem:
A developer builds an MCP tool `generate_image` that returns an image file. The tool result content type must be set correctly for Claude to process the image inline.

What content type should the tool result use for binary image data?

options.text: Return the image as a base64-encoded string with content type `text/plain`
Return the image as a URL string with content type `text/plain` and let Claude fetch it separately
Return the image as a base64-encoded blob with content type `image/png` (or appropriate MIME type)
MCP tools can only return text — image data must be returned as a file path

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because base64 with `text/plain` content type prevents the client from recognising the data as an image.

**B** is incorrect because returning a URL requires Claude to make a separate fetch, and the tool result itself would not contain the image data for inline processing.

**C** is correct because MCP tool results support multiple content types including image content. Returning a base64-encoded blob with the correct MIME type (e.g., `image/png`) allows the client to render and process the image inline.

**D** is incorrect because MCP tools can return image content — they are not limited to text only.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
A workflow assistant MCP server needs to provide Claude with a standardised checklist template for conducting quarterly business reviews. The checklist should be available as an invokable template that Claude can request to begin a structured QBR session.

Which MCP primitive is the best fit?

options.text: MCP Tool — define a `start_qbr_checklist` function that returns the template text
MCP Resource — expose the template as a document at a stable URI for Claude to fetch
MCP Prompt — define the QBR checklist as a reusable prompt template that injects structured context into the conversation
System prompt — embed the full QBR template in the application's system prompt

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a Tool invocation implies a function with potential side effects. Using a tool to return a static template is technically possible but semantically incorrect — there are no side effects.

**B** is incorrect because a Resource is addressable read-only data, typically fetched when needed. It is not designed to inject structured conversation context at invocation time.

**C** is correct because MCP Prompts are designed exactly for this use case — reusable conversation templates that inject structured instructions when invoked. The QBR checklist template becomes a standard workflow that Claude can invoke to begin a structured session.

**D** is incorrect because embedding in the application's system prompt couples the template to the application code and makes it unavailable through the standardised MCP interface.

[MCP: Prompts](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/prompts)

questions.stem:
An MCP server is accessed by two different client applications: a web dashboard and a mobile app. Both use the same MCP server. The web dashboard requires authentication via API key; the mobile app uses OAuth 2.0 tokens.

How should the MCP server handle multiple authentication methods?

options.text: Pick one authentication method and require all clients to use it
Implement authentication at the transport layer (HTTP headers), allowing different clients to present credentials in their respective formats; the MCP server validates each
Remove authentication entirely — MCP servers are internal and do not need security
Use separate MCP servers for each client application

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because requiring all clients to use the same authentication method forces one or both clients to change their authentication approach unnecessarily.

**B** is correct because MCP authentication is implemented at the transport layer. The MCP server can inspect incoming HTTP headers for API keys or OAuth tokens and validate each using the appropriate method. This allows multiple clients with different authentication methods to use the same server.

**C** is incorrect because removing authentication creates a security vulnerability — any caller could access the MCP server's tools and resources.

**D** is incorrect because maintaining separate servers for each client duplicates the MCP server implementation unnecessarily.

[MCP: Getting started](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro)

questions.stem:
A multimodal pipeline processes contracts that each include 3 page images (each ~500 tokens) and 1,500 tokens of text. The model has a 200,000-token context window.

Approximately how many contracts can be processed in a single API call?

options.text: Approximately 10 contracts (text only; images are not counted against the context window)
Approximately 133 contracts (200,000 ÷ 1,500 tokens each)
Approximately 67 contracts (200,000 ÷ 3,000 tokens per contract: 1,500 text + 3 × 500 image)
Unlimited — the model compresses images before counting them

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because images DO consume context window tokens. Images are not free — they are converted to a token count based on their dimensions and detail level.

**B** is incorrect because this calculation ignores the image tokens. Each contract is 1,500 text + 3 × 500 image = 3,000 tokens total.

**C** is correct because the full token budget per contract is 1,500 (text) + 1,500 (3 images × 500 each) = 3,000 tokens. The 200,000-token window accommodates approximately 200,000 ÷ 3,000 ≈ 67 contracts (with some budget reserved for instructions and output).

**D** is incorrect because the model does not compress images before counting tokens — each image contributes its full token cost to the context window.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A long-running customer support conversation has accumulated 50 turns. The earliest turns cover an issue that was resolved 40 turns ago. The agent now has a new unrelated issue to address.

Which context management approach preserves relevant context while reducing token count?

options.text: Keep all 50 turns — prior context may contain useful background information
Discard all context older than 10 turns without summarising
Summarise the resolved issue into a 1-2 sentence summary and remove the 40 turns of detail, keeping the summary and the last 10 turns in context
Start a completely new conversation without carrying any prior context

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because keeping all 50 turns grows context quadratically across sessions and keeps irrelevant resolved content competing with the current issue.

**B** is incorrect because silently discarding older turns without summarisation loses information that may have introduced constraints or decisions relevant to the current interaction.

**C** is correct because summarising the resolved issue into a brief note preserves the key facts (resolution, commitments made) while removing the verbose detail. The last 10 turns provide recent context. This compresses without information loss.

**D** is incorrect because discarding all prior context loses any account-level context, user preferences, or commitments made in earlier turns.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A developer plans an agentic task that will take approximately 100 tool calls. Each tool response averages 1,000 tokens. The system prompt is 5,000 tokens. The model has a 100,000-token context window.

Which context management strategy must be in place before the task begins?

options.text: No strategy needed — 100 × 1,000 + 5,000 = 105,000 tokens fits in the 100,000-token window
Plan to start a new session after every 50 tool calls
Implement progressive summarisation: periodically compress older tool results into summaries to keep total context under the window limit throughout the task
Increase max_tokens to expand the context window to 150,000 tokens

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 105,000 tokens exceeds the 100,000-token window — the task will fail without a strategy. Also, each turn adds both a tool call and the model response, making the actual context growth higher than estimated.

**B** is incorrect because starting a new session after 50 calls loses the accumulated task state needed for the remaining 50 calls.

**C** is correct because progressive summarisation compresses older tool results into condensed summaries as the context grows, keeping the running total within the window throughout the task. This is the standard strategy for long agentic runs.

**D** is incorrect because max_tokens controls output length, not input context capacity. The context window size is a model property, not a request parameter.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A team uses prompt caching for a 20,000-token legal knowledge base. Their system prompt structure is:
1. Legal knowledge base (20,000 tokens)
2. System instructions (500 tokens)
3. User query (varies)

They place `cache_control: ephemeral` at the end of the system instructions.

Which portion of the prompt gets cached?

options.text: Only the 500-token system instructions
Everything from the start up to and including the cache_control breakpoint: the 20,000-token knowledge base + 500-token instructions = 20,500 tokens
Only the user query portion, since it changes with each request
The entire prompt including the user query

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `cache_control` marks the end of the prefix to cache, not just the immediately preceding block.

**B** is correct because prompt caching caches all tokens from the start of the prompt up to the `cache_control` breakpoint. By placing it at the end of the system instructions (after the knowledge base + instructions), the entire 20,500-token prefix is cached. Subsequent requests with the same prefix pay cache-read pricing for those tokens.

**C** is incorrect because the user query changes per request and is not cached — it is the portion that varies.

**D** is incorrect because caching the user query would defeat the purpose — the query changes with every request.

[Prompt caching - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching)

questions.stem: A Claude API application calls Claude with tools. The API response contains `stop_reason: "tool_use"`. What does this indicate and what should the application do next?

options.text: Claude has finished the task; extract the final answer from the response content
An error occurred during tool processing; retry the request
Claude wants to invoke one or more tools; extract the tool_use blocks, execute them, and return the results as tool_result messages
The context window is full; summarise the conversation and start a new session

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `stop_reason: "tool_use"` explicitly signals that Claude is not done — it is waiting for tool results.

**B** is incorrect because `tool_use` stop reason is not an error; it is a normal part of the agentic loop.

**C** is correct because `stop_reason: "tool_use"` means Claude has generated tool_use content blocks requesting one or more tool invocations. The application must execute those tools and return the results as `tool_result` blocks before Claude can continue.

**D** is incorrect because `tool_use` is unrelated to context window limits.

[Agents - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/agents)

questions.stem:
An agentic loop runs until Claude returns `stop_reason: "end_turn"`. After 200 iterations, the loop is still running because Claude keeps returning `tool_use` stop reasons, but all tool calls are returning empty results.

What is the most likely problem and the correct fix?

options.text: The max_tokens is too low — increase it so Claude can finish the task in one response
The agent lacks a termination condition; add a maximum iteration count and break the loop when it is reached
The tool_use blocks are malformed; validate them before execution
Empty tool results are not valid; return an error to Claude instead of an empty result

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because max_tokens affects response length, not the number of tool call iterations.

**B** is correct because an agentic loop without a maximum iteration guard can run indefinitely. Adding a cap (e.g., 50 iterations) and breaking with an appropriate message when reached is the standard safeguard against runaway loops.

**C** is incorrect because malformed tool_use blocks would produce API errors, not repeated empty results.

**D** is incorrect because empty results are valid tool outcomes. An empty search result is different from an error. The problem is the loop, not the result format.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A financial reporting pipeline uses a hub-and-spoke architecture. The hub (coordinator) receives a request for a quarterly report and dispatches subtasks to a data-retrieval agent, a calculation agent, and a formatting agent.

What is the coordinator's primary responsibility in this architecture?

options.text: Execute the tool calls that each spoke agent requests
Break down the goal into subtasks, route each to the appropriate spoke, and synthesise the results into the final output
Provide the system prompt for each spoke agent based on the report type
Validate that the spoke agents' outputs conform to the required JSON schema

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because spoke agents execute their own tool calls. The coordinator orchestrates, not executes.

**B** is correct because in a hub-and-spoke architecture the coordinator's role is: decompose the goal into discrete subtasks, dispatch each subtask to the appropriate specialist agent, and synthesise the returned results into the final deliverable. This is the defining function of the hub.

**C** is incorrect because system prompt generation for spoke agents is a configuration concern, not a runtime coordination responsibility.

**D** is incorrect because while output validation is a sound practice, it is a secondary responsibility. The primary role is decomposition and synthesis.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team deploys a multi-agent system where each subagent is given a copy of the full system state at invocation. The state includes 50KB of configuration data, 30KB of user history, and 10KB of task-specific context.

Which context design reduces cost and latency without sacrificing task performance?

options.text: Give every subagent all 90KB so no information is lost
Give each subagent only the task-specific context it needs for its assigned subtask
Compress the 90KB state to a 1KB summary and give all subagents the same summary
Store all 90KB in the system prompt and use prompt caching to reduce cost

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because providing all 90KB to every subagent increases input token cost proportional to the number of agents without improving task performance if each agent only uses a fraction.

**B** is correct because scoping each subagent's context to what it actually needs reduces input tokens and cost while maintaining full task performance. A formatting agent does not need user history; a retrieval agent does not need configuration data.

**C** is incorrect because aggressive compression may discard information that some subagents need, harming task accuracy.

**D** is incorrect because prompt caching helps when the same content is reused across calls, but does not reduce the per-agent cost of sending the same 90KB to multiple agents simultaneously.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A coding agent writes code, runs tests, observes failures, and revises the code in a loop. After six iterations, all tests pass. The agent is about to commit 47 changed files.

Which action best reflects safe agentic practice before committing?

options.text: Commit immediately — the passing tests prove correctness
Run the linter on the changed files and fix any warnings before committing
Review the diff for unintended changes (deleted tests, disabled assertions, hardcoded values) before committing 47 files
Ask the user to manually approve every changed file individually before committing

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because tests passing does not guarantee no unintended side effects — tests can be deleted or disabled as an easy way to 'pass'.

**B** is incorrect because linting checks style, not whether the agent made semantically unintended changes like disabling assertions.

**C** is correct because reviewing the diff for unintended changes — deleted tests, disabled assertions, hardcoded values that make tests pass trivially — is the critical safety check before a large multi-file commit. This is especially important after many iteration cycles where an agent might have taken shortcuts.

**D** is incorrect because manual per-file approval of 47 files is impractical. A diff review is the appropriate checkpoint.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team configures a PostToolUse hook in Claude Code that runs after every Write tool call. The hook validates that the written file passes `eslint`. If the lint check fails, the hook exits non-zero.

What happens when the hook exits non-zero?

options.text: The file write is rolled back and Claude receives an error message to fix the linting issues
Claude Code terminates the session and reports a critical failure
The hook output is logged but the file write proceeds — PostToolUse hooks cannot block writes
The hook result is ignored because Claude Code does not support PostToolUse hooks

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a PostToolUse hook exiting non-zero signals Claude Code that the tool's result should be treated as a failure. Claude receives the hook's stderr as an error message and can use it to diagnose and fix the linting issues before continuing.

**B** is incorrect because a hook failure does not terminate the session — it provides feedback to Claude for correction.

**C** is incorrect because PostToolUse hooks do affect Claude's behaviour when they exit non-zero — the non-zero exit is explicitly how hooks communicate failures back to Claude.

**D** is incorrect because PostToolUse is a supported hook event in Claude Code.

[Claude Code Hooks](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks)

questions.stem:
An orchestrator agent assigns a task to a subagent, which returns a result claiming it found 500 matching records. The orchestrator passes this result directly to a report-generation agent without verification. Later, the report is found to contain fabricated record details.

Which design flaw enabled the fabricated data to reach the report?

options.text: The subagent had too many tools, causing it to call the wrong one
The orchestrator trusted the subagent's output without cross-checking it against the underlying data source
The report-generation agent lacked sufficient context about what constitutes valid data
The task decomposition was incorrect — report generation should be a subagent of the data-retrieval agent

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because tool count is unrelated to output verification.

**B** is correct because the orchestrator passed through an unverified claim (500 matching records) to the downstream agent. A well-designed orchestrator validates subagent outputs — checking record counts against actual data queries, not just accepting self-reported numbers — before propagating results.

**C** is incorrect because the report-generation agent's context is not the source of the problem. The fabricated data came from the first subagent's unverified claim.

**D** is incorrect because task decomposition structure does not cause fabrication — the lack of output verification does.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A workflow chains three Claude calls: the first generates a marketing email, the second translates it to Spanish, and the third checks tone consistency. The team wants to add a human review step after translation but before the tone check.

Where in the chain should the human-in-the-loop step be inserted?

options.text: Before the first call — the human should approve the strategy before any content is generated
After translation, before the tone check — the human reviews the Spanish email at the point where an error would propagate to downstream steps
After all three calls — the human approves the final output before it is sent
Randomly between any two calls to ensure unpredictability prevents model gaming

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because reviewing a strategy before content is generated gives the human less specific information to act on.

**B** is correct because inserting human review after the translation and before the tone check places oversight at the exact point where a translation error would otherwise propagate. Catching a mistranslation here is cheaper than catching it after downstream processing — and aligns with the stated requirement.

**C** is incorrect because final review catches errors only after all downstream processing has already run, wasting work if the translation was poor.

**D** is incorrect because random checkpoint placement has no design rationale and adds unpredictability without benefit.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A developer adds a CLAUDE.md at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` with the line 'Always use British English spelling.' A project at `/work/api/` has its own CLAUDE.md with 'Use American English spelling.'

Which instruction takes precedence when Claude Code is running inside `/work/api/`?

options.text: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` — user settings always override project settings
The project-level CLAUDE.md — more specific, closer-scope instructions take precedence over global ones
Neither — conflicting instructions cancel each other and Claude uses its default
Both — Claude applies both spellings randomly depending on the turn

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because user-level CLAUDE.md provides global defaults, but project-level instructions override them for that specific project.

**B** is correct because CLAUDE.md instructions follow a hierarchical override: project-level and subdirectory-level instructions are more specific and take precedence over the global user-level CLAUDE.md. American English would be used inside `/work/api/`.

**C** is incorrect because conflicting instructions do not cancel — the more specific (project-level) instruction wins.

**D** is incorrect because Claude does not randomly alternate between competing instructions.

[Claude Code: Memory](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory)

questions.stem: A team defines a project-level slash command at `.claude/commands/deploy.md`. A developer also has a user-level command with the same name at `~/.claude/commands/deploy.md`. When the developer types `/deploy`, which command runs?

options.text: Both commands run in sequence — project first, then user
The user-level command — user commands have higher priority
The project-level command — project scope is more specific and takes precedence
Claude Code prompts the developer to choose which one to run

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because commands with the same name do not both run — one takes precedence.

**B** is incorrect because project-level commands are more specific to the current working context and take precedence over user-level commands with the same name.

**C** is correct because project-level slash commands defined in `.claude/commands/` take precedence over user-level commands with the same name, following the same scope hierarchy as CLAUDE.md files.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code does not prompt for selection — precedence is determined by scope hierarchy.

[Claude Code Slash Commands](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/slash-commands)

questions.stem:
A team wants to add Claude Code to their CI pipeline to automatically generate release notes from merged PR descriptions. The pipeline passes the PR data via stdin and needs the output written to `RELEASE_NOTES.md` without any interactive prompts.

Which command invocation is correct?

options.text: `claude --batch` with the PR data file as an argument
`claude --ci-mode` piped from stdin with stdout redirected to the file
Pipe the PR data to `claude -p 'Generate release notes from these PR descriptions'` and redirect stdout to `RELEASE_NOTES.md`
Use `claude --auto-approve` to skip all confirmations and run in the background

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `--batch` is not a recognised Claude Code flag for CI mode.

**B** is incorrect because `--ci-mode` is not a recognised Claude Code flag.

**C** is correct because the `-p` (print) flag runs Claude Code non-interactively: it reads stdin as context, processes the prompt argument, prints the result to stdout, and exits. Redirecting stdout to `RELEASE_NOTES.md` completes the CI workflow.

**D** is incorrect because `--auto-approve` is not a recognised flag, and background execution does not address the non-interactive requirement.

[Claude Code GitHub Actions](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/github-actions)

questions.stem:
A company stores API secrets in environment variables. A developer configures an MCP server in `.mcp.json` with: `"apiKey": "sk-live-abc123"`. A security audit finds this a month later.

What went wrong and how should it be fixed?

options.text: The key was hardcoded instead of using `${API_KEY}` environment variable expansion; fix by replacing the hardcoded value with the expansion syntax
The `.mcp.json` file should not be in the repo at all; delete it
MCP servers do not support API keys; the authentication method must be changed
The key was in the wrong JSON field; move it to the `secrets` array

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `.mcp.json` supports `${ENV_VAR}` expansion for secrets. The key should be `"apiKey": "${MY_API_KEY}"` where `MY_API_KEY` is set in the developer's environment. This keeps the secret out of the config file and out of version control.

**B** is incorrect because `.mcp.json` is a legitimate project configuration file that should be version-controlled — but without hardcoded secrets.

**C** is incorrect because MCP servers do support API key authentication; the issue is how the key is stored, not the authentication method.

**D** is incorrect because `.mcp.json` does not have a `secrets` array; environment variable expansion is the correct mechanism.

[Claude Code Settings](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/settings)

questions.stem:
A bug classification pipeline labels issues as Critical, High, Medium, or Low. After deployment, the team finds that 60% of Medium issues are incorrectly classified as High. The system prompt says: 'High means the feature is broken for many users.'

Which system prompt change most directly fixes this?

options.text: Add 'Be conservative with High labels' to the system prompt
Define explicit quantitative criteria: 'High: >1,000 users affected with no workaround. Medium: 100-1,000 users OR a workaround exists' — with one example of each
Switch from four severity levels to two: Critical and Non-critical
Add a confidence score field and route any High-labeled issues above 0.8 confidence to human review

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 'be conservative' is vague — it does not tell Claude where the High/Medium boundary is.

**B** is correct because replacing vague definitions with explicit quantitative criteria gives Claude an unambiguous decision boundary. Including one example of each level further calibrates Claude on borderline cases.

**C** is incorrect because reducing to two categories loses the granularity the team needs to prioritise work.

**D** is incorrect because routing based on confidence does not fix the underlying classification error — it adds overhead while the wrong boundaries remain.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
A team detects that their content moderation classifier is flagging 40% of legitimate cooking recipe posts as 'potentially harmful' due to language about cutting, slicing, and heat. The false positive rate is disrupting operations.

Which targeted fix addresses this without broadly reducing sensitivity?

options.text: Lower the classification threshold for all categories to reduce the overall false positive rate
Remove the 'potentially harmful' category from the classifier entirely
Add a domain-detection step: if the content is identified as a recipe, skip or adjust the harmful-content check for culinary terms
Increase temperature so the model explores more classification options before committing

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because lowering the threshold for all categories reduces true positives as well as false positives, harming overall classifier effectiveness.

**B** is incorrect because removing the category eliminates legitimate harmful content detection.

**C** is correct because a domain-detection step applies context-aware logic: if the content is a recipe, culinary terms like 'cut' and 'heat' are harmless. This reduces false positives in the known problematic domain without broadly weakening detection.

**D** is incorrect because higher temperature increases output variability, which worsens classification consistency.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A pipeline extracts contract clauses and must include a `governing_law` field in every output. When the field is absent from the source document, Claude sometimes omits it from the output and sometimes hallucinates a value.

Which schema design prevents hallucination while ensuring the field is always present?

options.text: Mark `governing_law` as required in the schema but with no type constraint
Mark `governing_law` as an optional field that Claude can omit if not found
Mark `governing_law` as required but nullable, with instructions to return null when the field is not present in the source
Add 'Never omit governing_law' to the system prompt

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a required field with no type constraint still pushes Claude to populate it with something, which may lead to hallucination when the value is absent.

**B** is incorrect because an optional field allows Claude to omit it, which the team does not want — the field must always appear.

**C** is correct because a required but nullable field combined with the instruction 'return null when not found' gives Claude a legitimate, non-hallucinated value to use when the source lacks the field. The field always appears in output (required), and null signals absence without fabricating data.

**D** is incorrect because a text instruction to 'never omit' still leaves Claude with no sanctioned value when the field is genuinely absent, which can push it toward hallucination.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A team calls the Message Batches API with 5,000 requests. Each request includes a `custom_id`. Twenty-four hours later, they retrieve the results. The results arrive in a different order than the requests.

How should the team correlate results to the original requests?

options.text: Sort results by timestamp — the API guarantees FIFO processing order
Use the `custom_id` field in each result to match it to the corresponding input request
Re-submit the batch and specify `preserve_order: true`
Infer order from the response content — each response includes the original request text

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Message Batches API does not guarantee FIFO processing order.

**B** is correct because `custom_id` is the designed correlation mechanism. Each result carries the same `custom_id` that was set in the corresponding input request, allowing the team to reliably match results to inputs regardless of processing order.

**C** is incorrect because `preserve_order: true` is not a parameter in the Message Batches API.

**D** is incorrect because results do not include the original request text — they reference the request only through `custom_id`.

[Message Batches API - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/message-batches)

questions.stem:
An agentic pipeline processes a financial report using multiple tool calls. Each tool returns a large JSON payload (~8KB). After 15 tool calls, the context window is nearly full and subsequent responses degrade in quality.

Which context management strategy best preserves task performance?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to extend how much Claude can output per turn
Summarise or truncate each tool result before appending it to the conversation, retaining only the information needed for the current task
Remove all previous tool results from the context and let Claude re-request them if needed
Split the task across two separate Claude sessions and merge results manually

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because max_tokens controls output length, not input (context) capacity.

**B** is correct because trimming or summarising large tool results before appending them to the conversation dramatically reduces context growth. Retaining only the relevant subset of each 8KB payload keeps the context window sustainable across many tool calls.

**C** is incorrect because removing all previous tool results forces Claude to re-invoke tools for information it has already retrieved, wasting latency and tokens.

**D** is incorrect because splitting into two sessions creates a coordination problem and requires manual merging.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A Claude integration has 35 tools available. Users report that Claude frequently calls the wrong tool — selecting `get_account_details` instead of `get_account_balance` for balance inquiries, and vice versa.

What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: 35 tools exceeds the maximum supported by the API; remove tools until fewer than 20 remain
The tool descriptions for `get_account_details` and `get_account_balance` are too similar; Claude cannot distinguish when to use each
The tools are named too similarly; rename one to something completely different
Claude ignores tool names and selects tools by their position in the list

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 35 tools does not exceed an API limit, though it can increase cognitive load on the model.

**B** is correct because when two tool descriptions have overlapping language — 'retrieves account information' vs. 'gets account data' — Claude struggles to determine which tool matches the user's intent. The fix is to make descriptions unambiguous: 'get_account_balance: Returns the current numeric balance and currency only' vs. 'get_account_details: Returns account metadata including owner name, creation date, and status — not the current balance.'

**C** is incorrect because renaming without fixing the descriptions does not resolve the semantic overlap problem.

**D** is incorrect because Claude selects tools based on descriptions and names, not list position.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
An MCP server returns a 15,000-token JSON payload as a tool result. Claude's context window is 200,000 tokens. After the tool call, Claude's response degrades: it stops referencing earlier conversation turns and loses track of the original task.

What is the most effective fix?

options.text: Increase the context window limit to 500,000 tokens
Ask Claude to process the tool result in a separate session
Have the MCP server return a compact summary of the key information rather than the full raw payload
Re-order the conversation so the tool result appears at the beginning of the context

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the problem is not a hard context limit — at 15,000 tokens the window is not full. The issue is that a large tool result pushes earlier context further back, reducing its effective influence.

**B** is incorrect because using a separate session loses access to the original task context.

**C** is correct because a compact, structured summary from the MCP server delivers the essential information in far fewer tokens. This reduces context pressure and allows earlier task context to remain influential throughout the conversation.

**D** is incorrect because re-ordering a tool result before the conversation history is not possible without restructuring the conversation, and would destroy conversational coherence.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A developer builds an MCP tool called `search_inventory` that returns up to 2,000 product records per call. Claude frequently exceeds context limits because the full record list is appended to the conversation.

Which tool design change most directly solves this?

options.text: Reduce the tool's result limit from 2,000 to 200 records
Change the tool to return paginated results with a `cursor` parameter and a `max_results` field that defaults to 20
Have the tool compress the records to base64 before returning them
Return results as a resource URL that Claude can fetch separately instead of inline data

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because reducing to 200 records reduces the problem but does not solve it architecturally — large results will still fill context for complex queries.

**B** is correct because pagination with a configurable `max_results` default allows Claude to request small batches (e.g., 20 at a time), keeping each tool result small. Claude iterates with cursor values to retrieve subsequent pages when needed, rather than receiving a 2,000-record payload that floods the context.

**C** is incorrect because base64 encoding does not reduce token count — base64 is larger than the original text.

**D** is incorrect because returning a URL adds a fetch step and does not reduce the total data that eventually enters the context; it only defers the problem.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)
